description: The party stew. Beef, tripe, ponmo, kidney, and chicken all in one deeply fried tomato base. Every cut brings a different texture and flavour — the complexity is the point. This is the stew people ask for the recipe.

ingredients.name: beef chunks (chuck or brisket)
ingredients.note: The base meat. Chuck or brisket — tough cuts that become tender and rich after long cooking.

ingredients.name: beef tripe (shaki)
ingredients.note: Takes the longest to cook. Must be parboiled separately until fully tender before joining the stew. Has a distinctive chewy texture that many love.

ingredients.name: ponmo (cow skin)
ingredients.note: Must be pre-cooked until soft. Absorbs stew flavour beautifully and adds a gelatinous richness to the sauce.

ingredients.name: beef kidney
ingredients.note: Cook separately — brief parboil, then fry. Strong flavour that enriches the base. Remove the white membrane before cooking.

ingredients.name: chicken pieces (thighs/drumsticks)
ingredients.note: Adds a lighter contrast to the heavy beef cuts. Parboil separately as it cooks faster.

ingredients.name: plum tomatoes
ingredients.note: More tomatoes for a larger, more complex stew. Fresh is best.

ingredients.name: red bell pepper (tatashe)
ingredients.note: More tatashe for deeper colour and sweetness to balance the rich meats.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Assorted stew can handle more heat — the fat from multiple meats absorbs it.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 each for parboiling beef, tripe, and chicken separately, 1 sliced for the stew base.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: Larger quantity for a bigger stew. The assorted meats will also render fat into the pot.

ingredients.name: tomato paste
ingredients.note: More paste for a richer, more concentrated base to carry all the different meat flavours.

ingredients.name: curry powder
ingredients.note: Divided — some for marinating the meats, some for the stew base.

ingredients.name: thyme
ingredients.note: Divided between meat marinades and stew base.

ingredients.name: bay leaves
ingredients.note: Particularly important in assorted stew — adds herbal depth that cuts through the richness of multiple meats.

ingredients.name: seasoning cubes
ingredients.note: Distributed across all the parboiling stages and the stew base.

ingredients.name: garlic
ingredients.note: For marinating the beef and tripe. Highly recommended — the assorted meats can carry the strong garlic flavour.

ingredients.name: ginger
ingredients.note: Particularly helps with the tripe and kidney smell. Use generously in those marinades.

ingredients.name: white pepper
ingredients.note: For the meat marinades.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: Essential for depth in assorted stew — it bridges all the different meat flavours.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season each meat separately during parboiling. Season the stew base carefully at the end — the collective stock is very salty.

ingredients.name: combined meat stock
ingredients.note: Combine the stocks from all the different meats. This blended stock is the most flavourful liquid you can put in a stew.

steps.title: Prepare and Marinate All Meats
steps.instruction: This step requires patience and organisation — you are handling five different proteins at once, each needing its own treatment. Clean all meats separately and thoroughly: scrub the tripe with salt and lime juice until it smells completely neutral, remove the white membrane and any tubes from the kidney and soak it in salted water for 10 minutes, and rinse the beef and ponmo under cold water. Season each meat type in its own bowl — beef and tripe get the full marinade treatment with curry, thyme, garlic, ginger, white pepper, a crushed seasoning cube, and salt; chicken gets curry, thyme, and seasoning; kidney gets just salt and a touch of white pepper to keep its flavour clean. Let everything rest for at least 30 minutes, longer if you have time.
micro_steps: Set out five separate bowls or plates — one for each meat type
Scrub the tripe thoroughly with coarse salt and lime or lemon juice, rinse under cold running water, and repeat until it smells completely neutral
Remove the white membrane and any visible tubes from the kidney — use a small knife and work carefully
Soak the cleaned kidney in lightly salted cold water for 10 minutes, then drain and pat dry
Rinse beef chunks and ponmo under cold water and pat dry with paper towels
Season beef and tripe: combine 1 tsp curry powder, 1 tsp thyme, all the minced garlic, grated ginger, ½ tsp white pepper, 1 crushed seasoning cube, and salt — rub into each cut thoroughly
Season chicken pieces with 1 tsp curry powder, ½ tsp thyme, 1 crushed seasoning cube, and a pinch of salt
Season kidney pieces lightly with just salt and a pinch of white pepper — do not over-season it at this stage
Cover all bowls with cling wrap or a plate and rest at room temperature for at least 30 minutes — 1 hour is better
While meats are resting, blend your tomatoes, tatashe, and scotch bonnets and set aside — you will need it later
prep_notes: Have all five proteins fully thawed and at room temperature before you start — cold meat takes longer at every stage
Prepare and label five separate bowls before touching any of the meat — organisation is the difference between a great assorted stew and a chaotic one
Mince the garlic and grate the ginger before you start handling raw meat so your hands stay clean during seasoning
Blend the tomato base (tomatoes, tatashe, scotch bonnet) and set aside before cooking begins — things move fast once the heat is on
steps.sensory_cue: All meats should smell clean, fresh, and well-seasoned after marinating. The tripe especially must smell completely neutral — no ammonia, no barnyard smell. If it still smells off, wash it again with more salt and lime before proceeding.
steps.failure_risk: Improperly cleaned tripe or kidney will give the entire finished stew an off-putting, funky smell that no amount of seasoning can cover. Take the cleaning step seriously — it is not optional. Season each meat type separately because they have different flavour intensities and cooking times; mixing them now causes problems later.

steps.title: Parboil Tripe Separately (Longest Cook)
steps.instruction: The tripe goes on first because it takes significantly longer than every other component — start it before anything else to manage your total cook time. Place the seasoned tripe in a pot and cover with water, adding a chopped onion and a bay leaf for flavour. Bring to a boil over high heat, then reduce to a steady simmer. Cook until the tripe is completely tender all the way through — it should yield to a spoon, cut easily with no resistance, and have a slightly translucent, soft appearance. This takes 60–90 minutes depending on the thickness of your tripe. Once done, remove from the pot, allow to cool slightly, then cut into bite-sized pieces. Reserve every drop of the stock — it is intensely flavoured and will form part of your combined stock later.
micro_steps: Place the seasoned tripe pieces into a medium pot — do not overcrowd, use a large pot if needed
Add 1 roughly chopped onion and 1 bay leaf directly into the pot
Pour in enough cold water to fully cover the tripe by about 3cm
Bring to a rolling boil over high heat — this takes about 8–10 minutes
Once boiling, reduce heat to a steady medium simmer
Cook uncovered for 60–90 minutes, checking every 20 minutes and topping up with hot water if the level drops below the tripe
At the 60-minute mark, press a piece of tripe firmly with the back of a spoon — if it offers resistance, continue cooking
Test again every 10 minutes until a piece cuts easily with a spoon and feels uniformly soft throughout
Remove tripe from the pot and set aside to cool for 5 minutes
Cut the cooled tripe into bite-sized pieces — about 3–4cm — and set aside
Pour the tripe stock through a sieve into a large bowl and reserve — do not discard it
steps.sensory_cue: Properly cooked tripe is uniformly soft, slightly translucent at the edges, and cuts cleanly with no rubbery resistance. The stock will be very dark, strongly flavoured, and smell earthy and rich — almost gamey in the best possible way.
steps.failure_risk: Undercooked tripe is the most common mistake in assorted stew — it will be unpleasantly tough and rubbery in the final dish, and there is no way to fix it once the stew is assembled. The tripe must be completely tender before it joins anything else. If in doubt, give it another 15 minutes.

steps.title: Parboil Beef and Ponmo Together
steps.instruction: While the tripe is cooking, get the beef and ponmo going in a second pot. Place them together with a chopped onion, a bay leaf, and just enough water to barely cover — you want concentrated stock, not a diluted broth. Cook on medium-high heat. The ponmo will soften first — typically around 30–40 minutes — so check it regularly and remove it the moment it is completely soft and gelatinous. Let the beef continue cooking until it is about 75% tender, meaning it yields to a fork but still has just a little resistance in the centre. Reserve the combined beef and ponmo stock.
micro_steps: Place beef chunks and diced ponmo together in a separate pot
Add 1 roughly chopped onion and 1 bay leaf
Add just enough cold water to barely cover — about 2–3cm above the meat
Bring to a boil over high heat, then reduce to medium-high
After 25 minutes, start checking the ponmo — press a piece firmly between your fingers (carefully — it is hot)
Remove ponmo from the pot the moment it feels completely soft and slightly gelatinous — do not let it overcook
Set the ponmo aside in a bowl
Continue simmering the beef for another 15–25 minutes
Test the beef: pierce the thickest piece with a fork — it should yield with slight resistance, not fall apart
Remove beef from the pot and set aside
Pour the stock through a sieve into a bowl and reserve alongside the tripe stock
steps.sensory_cue: Beef at 75% done: yields to a fork but pushes back slightly at the centre. Ponmo when ready: completely soft, slightly gelatinous, and slightly translucent — almost jelly-like. The combined stock should be rich, dark, and smell deeply savoury.
steps.failure_risk: Ponmo overcooks and disintegrates faster than you expect. Once it reaches that soft, gelatinous stage, remove it immediately — even 5 extra minutes can cause it to fall apart completely in the stew later. Watch it closely after the 25-minute mark.

steps.title: Parboil Chicken and Fry Kidney
steps.instruction: Parboil the chicken pieces in a small amount of water with a little seasoning — you only need enough liquid to generate steam, not a full broth, as the chicken cooks much faster than the other cuts. Cook until about 80% done, meaning the juices run almost clear and the flesh is mostly cooked through but still has a little give at the bone. Reserve the stock. For the kidney, drain it from its soaking water, pat dry, and cut into 2–3cm chunks. Heat a splash of oil in a small pan over high heat until very hot and fry the kidney pieces for exactly 2 minutes per side — you want a good brown sear on the outside while the inside remains slightly pink, as it will finish cooking in the stew.
micro_steps: Place chicken pieces into a small pot — thighs and drumsticks only, no breast
Add just enough water to come halfway up the chicken pieces — about 1 cup
Add a pinch of seasoning and bring to a boil over high heat
Reduce to medium and cook for 15–18 minutes
Pierce the thickest part of a thigh with a knife — juices should run almost clear with just a faint pink tinge
Remove chicken from pot, set aside, and reserve the stock
Drain the kidney from its soaking water and pat completely dry with paper towels — wet kidney will not sear properly
Cut kidney into 2–3cm chunks
Heat a splash of vegetable oil in a small frying pan over high heat until it shimmers
Add kidney pieces in a single layer — do not overcrowd, fry in two batches if needed
Fry for exactly 2 minutes on the first side without moving — you want a dark sear
Flip and fry for 2 minutes on the second side, then remove immediately to a plate
steps.sensory_cue: Chicken should look mostly cooked through with nearly clear juices. Kidney should have a dark-brown sear on the outside and smell savoury and meaty — not sharp or pungent. A faint pink interior is correct at this stage.
steps.failure_risk: Overcooked chicken at this stage will completely fall off the bone and disappear into the stew rather than being a distinct component. Pull it at 80% without hesitation. For the kidney, the 2-minute-per-side rule is firm — it turns rubbery and strong-tasting if overcooked.

steps.title: Fry All Meats
steps.instruction: This step is what separates assorted stew from a simple meat stew — the individual searing of each cut builds layers of caramelised flavour that infuse the entire base. Heat half the vegetable oil in your largest, heaviest pot over high heat until it is very hot and just beginning to shimmer. Fry the meats in batches in this order: beef first (it needs the most colour), then chicken, then tripe, then ponmo last (it burns fastest). Give each batch space in the pot — do not crowd — and fry until genuinely golden-brown on the outside. Remove each batch to a plate as it is done. Do not clean the pot between batches, and do not clean it after the last batch — that dark, caramelised fond coating the bottom is concentrated flavour gold.
micro_steps: Heat half the vegetable oil (about 6 tbsp) in your largest, heaviest pot over high heat
Wait until the oil is properly hot — it should shimmer and a drop of water will spit immediately
Add beef chunks in a single layer — do not stack or crowd — fry in two batches if needed
Fry beef for 3–4 minutes per side until deeply golden-brown, then remove to a plate
Add chicken pieces to the same oil and fry for 2–3 minutes per side until golden — remove to plate
Add tripe pieces and fry for 2–3 minutes, turning occasionally, until the edges brown and crisp slightly — remove to plate
Add ponmo last and fry for 1–2 minutes only, turning once — it colours and can burn very quickly
Remove ponmo and add the already-fried kidney pieces for just 30 seconds to warm through
Remove kidney and turn off the heat
Look at the bottom of the pot — it should be coated with dark, caramelised drippings from all the meats
Do not clean the pot — this fond is the foundation of your stew base
steps.sensory_cue: Every piece of meat should have a visible golden-brown exterior. The pot bottom should look dark and heavily caramelised — almost burnt-looking, but not acrid. The kitchen will smell extraordinary at this point — a deep, meaty, slightly smoky aroma that is unmistakably Nigerian party cooking.
steps.failure_risk: Skipping or rushing the individual searing step is the one thing that most clearly separates good assorted stew from great assorted stew. Every batch needs real contact time with the hot oil to develop colour. If you crowd the pot, the meat steams instead of searing and you lose all that flavour development.

steps.title: Build and Fry the Stew Base
steps.instruction: Without cleaning the pot — the meat drippings and fond are staying right where they are — add the remaining oil if the pot looks dry. Turn the heat to medium and fry the sliced onions directly in the drippings, scraping up the caramelised fond from the bottom as they cook, until they are deep golden. Add tomato paste and stir vigorously for 4 minutes until it darkens from bright red to a deep brick colour. Pour in the blended tomato and pepper mix, increase heat to high, and fry the entire base — stirring every 90 seconds — for 25–30 minutes. You need a much longer fry time here than in a single-protein stew because the volume of base is larger and it needs to be bold enough to carry five different meats.
micro_steps: Check the pot — if it looks dry, add the remaining vegetable oil; if there is plenty of rendered fat already, add just a splash
Turn heat to medium and add the sliced onion rings directly into the drippings
Stir immediately, scraping the caramelised fond from the bottom of the pot into the onions
Fry the onions for 5–6 minutes until they are deep golden and smell very sweet
Add 4 tablespoons of tomato paste and stir vigorously — the paste will try to stick
Fry the paste for 4–5 minutes, stirring constantly, until it darkens from bright orange-red to a deep brick-red
Pour in all the blended tomato, tatashe, and scotch bonnet mixture
Increase heat to high and stir everything together thoroughly
Fry the base over high heat, stirring every 90 seconds, for 25–30 minutes
The base is ready when it looks almost paste-like, very dark brick-red, and the oil has risen clearly and visibly to the surface
Taste the base — it should smell deeply fried, rich, and completely lacking any raw tomato sharpness
steps.sensory_cue: The finished base should look almost like a thick, dark paste — deep brick-red to dark orange-brown. Oil should be clearly floating on the surface. All the caramelised meat fond from the pot bottom should have dissolved completely into the base, giving it a darker, richer, more complex colour than a plain tomato base. The raw tomato smell should be entirely gone.
steps.failure_risk: This is the largest tomato base in any of these stew packs — it needs the full 25–30 minutes without cutting corners. A watery, under-fried base cannot carry the weight of five different proteins. The oil rising to the surface is your non-negotiable confirmation that the base is ready.

steps.title: Season and Add Combined Stock
steps.instruction: Reduce the heat to medium once the base is fully fried. Add the curry powder, thyme, ground crayfish, bay leaves, and crushed seasoning cubes, stirring each addition in thoroughly. Now taste the base before reaching for any salt — the combined stock you are about to add is already deeply seasoned from all the individual parboiling stages, and over-salting at this point cannot be undone. Pour in 2 cups of the combined meat stock — mix the tripe, beef, and chicken stocks together in the bowl before adding — and stir everything together. Bring the pot to a rolling simmer and taste again. The base at this point should be almost overwhelming in its depth and complexity.
micro_steps: Reduce heat to medium
Add 1 tsp curry powder and stir it into the base thoroughly
Add 1 tsp dried thyme and stir in
Add 2 tablespoons of ground crayfish and stir — the colour and aroma will shift noticeably
Add bay leaves and stir in
Crush the remaining seasoning cubes into powder and add — stir until fully dissolved
Taste the base now before adding any salt — assess how salty it already is
In a separate bowl, combine the reserved tripe stock, beef and ponmo stock, and chicken stock
Pour 2 cups of this combined stock into the pot
Stir everything together and bring to a rolling simmer over medium-high heat
Taste again — add salt only if genuinely needed, and only a small pinch at a time
The base should taste complex, rich, and deeply savoury — almost intense
steps.sensory_cue: The seasoned base with combined stock is the most flavourful liquid in any of these stew recipes. The layers — fried tomato, crayfish, spices, and the combined depth of multiple meat stocks — should be clearly distinct on your palate. It should taste almost too bold on its own; that is correct, as the meats will absorb and mellow it.
steps.failure_risk: Tasting before salting is not optional at this stage — it is the most critical quality control step in the entire recipe. The combined multi-meat stock is already heavily seasoned from three separate parboiling stages. Adding a full measure of salt without tasting first is almost certain to result in an oversalted stew with no remedy.

steps.title: Add All Meats and Finish
steps.instruction: Add the fried meats back into the stew in stages, starting with the cuts that need the most time to absorb flavour — beef first, then tripe, then ponmo, then kidney, and finally chicken last since it is the most delicate and can fall apart easily. Stir very gently after each addition using a folding motion from the bottom up rather than aggressive stirring. Cover the pot and let everything simmer together on low heat for 25–30 minutes, lifting the lid to fold gently every 8 minutes. The stew will thicken around all the meats and each piece will become deeply coated and glossy. Remove every bay leaf before serving, do a final salt check, and let the stew rest uncovered for 10 minutes before plating.
micro_steps: Reduce heat to low before adding any meat
Add beef chunks first and fold gently into the base — make sure each piece is coated
Add tripe pieces and fold in
Add ponmo pieces and fold in very gently — it is the most fragile at this stage
Add kidney pieces and fold in
Add chicken pieces last and fold in carefully — do not stir aggressively
Cover the pot with a lid
Set a timer for 8 minutes, then lift the lid and fold the stew once from the bottom up — gently
Cover again and repeat every 8 minutes for a total of 25–30 minutes
After 25 minutes, check the stew — it should be thick, glossy, and coating every piece of meat
If it looks too thick, add a small splash of the remaining stock; if too thin, leave the lid off for the final 5 minutes
Find and remove every single bay leaf
Do a final salt taste and adjust if needed
Turn off heat and rest uncovered for 10 minutes before serving
steps.sensory_cue: Every piece of meat should look deeply coated, glossy, and rich. The stew sauce itself should be thick but not stiff — it should coat the back of a spoon heavily. Each meat type should be clearly identifiable by its texture: beef tender and chunky, tripe chewy and soft, ponmo gelatinous, kidney firm, chicken yielding. The smell should be layered, bold, and unmistakably party food.
steps.failure_risk: Aggressive stirring at this stage is the enemy — it breaks apart the more delicate cuts, particularly the ponmo and chicken, turning them into shreds instead of distinct pieces. Use only slow, gentle folds. If the stew reduces too much and catches on the bottom, add a splash of the remaining stock immediately and reduce heat further. This stew genuinely tastes better after sitting for an hour, so if you can rest it longer, do.
